data.title: Amazon in charts: AWS revenue rises 28%
Federal Signal raises 2026 adjusted EPS outlook to $4.80-$5.05 while lifting SSG margin target to 22%-28%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Federal Signal (FSS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We are proud of our record-setting first quarter performance, which included new quarterly records across net sales, adjusted EPS and adjusted EBITDA,” said (President, CEO & Director Jennifer Sherman), adding that “broad-based strength across several product verticals,” “early progress… integrating Hog, New Way and Mega,” and “strong margin performance” drove “better-than-expected results versus our expectations.”
  • “We delivered strong financial results for the quarter with 35% year-over-year net sales growth, 52% operating income improvement, gross margin expansion, a 190 basis point improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin, robust cash generation and strong order intake,” said (Senior VP & CFO Ian Hudson), reporting “consolidated net sales for the quarter were $626 million,” “consolidated operating income… was $99.7 million,” and “consolidated adjusted EBITDA… was $126.3 million… [for] a margin of 20.2%.”
  • Hudson said “orders for the quarter were $623 million… contributing to a backlog at the end of the quarter of $1.04 billion,” and added, “we generated $101 million of cash from operations during the quarter.”

Outlook

  • “We are raising our full year adjusted EPS outlook to a new range of $4.80 to $5.05 from the prior range of $4.50 to $4.80,” Sherman said.
  • “We are also increasing our full year net sales outlook to a new range of between $2.57 billion and $2.66 billion from the prior range of between $2.55 billion and $2.65 billion,” Sherman said, while adding, “we are maintaining our CapEx outlook of between $45 million and $55 million for the year.”
  • On longer-term profitability priorities, Sherman said, “we are formally raising our EBITDA margin targets today for our Safety and Security Group to a new range of 22% to 28% from the previous range of 18% to 24%,” and added, “these margin targets represent through-cycle margin targets and do not present any sort of long-term ceiling.”

Financial results

  • Hudson reported “GAAP diluted EPS for the quarter was $1.14 per share,” and “on an adjusted basis, EPS for the quarter was $1.18 per share.”
  • Hudson said “consolidated gross margin for the quarter was 28.7%,” and added that “as a percentage of net sales, our selling, engineering, general and administrative expenses for the quarter were down 150 basis points from Q1 last year.”
  • On balance sheet and capital allocation, Hudson said, “we ended the quarter with $480 million of net debt and availability under our credit facility of $939 million,” and noted, “we paid dividends of $9.2 million during the quarter, reflecting an increased dividend of $0.15 per share.”

Q&A

  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: “the first quarter did not play out as the first quarter normally does… how should we read the first quarter… in the context of the full year?” (President, CEO & Director Sherman) responded, “our acquisitions did better than expected… particularly New Way,” and “SSG had a better quarter than expected,” adding, “we’re expecting our EPS contribution to be roughly evenly split by quarter.”
  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: asked about inflation and raw materials; (Senior VP & CFO Hudson) answered, “the major raw materials we have really primarily steel… we’ve locked [pricing]… through the rest of the year,” and added, “we will experience some inflation… in the second half of the year… that is considered in the guidance that we gave today.”
  • Ross Sparenblek, William Blair: asked about working-capital-driven cash flow; (Senior VP & CFO Hudson) said, “we ended the year probably at a higher level of working capital than necessary… there was an effort… to work down some of that,” adding, “we’re not expecting to be as dramatic as what we saw in Q1.”
  • Walter Liptak, Seaport: asked for order-growth details; (President, CEO & Director Sherman) said “orders are up 13%,” but added, “the underlying core organic orders… was down about $20 million… a reduction in international export orders,” and concluded, “excluding that, organic orders were flat.”
  • Christian Zyla, KeyBanc: asked about lead times and throughput; (President, CEO & Director Sherman) said, “overall, we saw kind of 15% year-over-year improvement,” and added, “our lead times now for our sewer cleaners are running about 11 months and for our street sweepers… a tick above a year.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral on key uncertainties, with direct prompts on unusual seasonality (“the first quarter did not play out as the first quarter normally does”), working-capital sustainability (“should we expect more of an inventory benefit”), and international demand (“give us more color as to what’s going on there”).
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing execution and raised targets, including “record-setting first quarter performance” (President, CEO & Director Sherman) and “we delivered strong financial results” (Senior VP & CFO Hudson).
  • Versus last quarter’s tone that emphasized seasonality (“we expect Q1 net sales and earnings to be lower than subsequent quarters,” said (President, CEO & Director Sherman) in Q4 2025), Q1 2026 messaging shifted to less seasonal earnings (“the seasonality of our earnings isn’t as pronounced this year,” said (President, CEO & Director Sherman)).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher quarter-over-quarter: in Q4 2025, Sherman said “net sales of between $2.55 billion and $2.65 billion and adjusted EPS between $4.50 and $4.80,” while in Q1 2026 she said “net sales… between $2.57 billion and $2.66 billion” and “adjusted EPS… $4.80 to $5.05.”
  • Strategy emphasis broadened from integrating acquisitions to scaling enterprise investments: in Q1 2026, Sherman said “we are also scaling several enterprise-wide investments starting in the second quarter of 2026,” including “data analytics and operations.”
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on orders/backlog and New Way, while Q1 2026 added deeper probing on cash-flow drivers and the sustainability of working-capital benefits.

Risks and concerns

  • Sherman said “an approximate $20 million year-over-year reduction in international export orders,” adding, “we are closely monitoring any political impacts on international demand stemming from current geopolitical conflicts.”
  • On tariffs, Sherman said, “we don’t have material exposure… we are mostly in country for country… [and] the biggest potential exposure is on the chassis side, which is a pass-through for us.”

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as “record-setting” and used the quarter’s results, cash generation, and acquisition integration progress to raise full-year 2026 guidance to $4.80-$5.05 in adjusted EPS and $2.57B-$2.66B in net sales, while also lifting SSG’s through-cycle EBITDA margin target range to 22%-28% and reaffirming plans to deliver $15M-$20M of New Way annual synergies by the end of 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elon Musk tells court he was a “fool” to fund OpenAI
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE) CEO Elon Musk told a federal court Wednesday that he was a “fool” to fund OpenAI (OPENAI) as a non-profit venture.

“I was a fool who provided them free funding to create a startup,” Musk said during his testimony on Wednesday, according to The Wall Street Journal. “I gave them $38 million of essentially free funding to create what would become an $800 billion company.”

Musk, a co-founder of OpenAI, is suing the company, along with CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman, for over $180B for allegedly manipulating him into investing in the AI startup as a non-profit project, only to later convert it into a for-profit entity. Musk has also asked that the court remove Altman and Brockman from their positions and unwind OpenAI's conversion into a for-profit company, the WSJ said.

OpenAI, meanwhile, has argued that Musk was supportive of plans to convert the company, but walked away when OpenAI's other co-founders refused to cede control to him. Musk, who went on to establish AI company xAI, has also been accused of filing the lawsuit to slow down OpenAI's progress, the WSJ added.

The WSJ also noted that evidence had been submitted indicating Musk had proposed rolling OpenAI into Tesla at one point. OpenAI is best known for its AI chatbot ChatGPT.

In his testimony, Musk said that while xAI was “technically competitive” with OpenAI, it was a “much smaller” operation. XAI, which developed the AI chatbot Grok, is now a division of SpaceX. 

Both OpenAI (OPENAI) and SpaceX (SPACEX) are reportedly planning massive IPOs for later this year. Microsoft (MSFT) is a major backer of OpenAI.

In addition to xAI, SpaceX also owns satellite internet provider Starlink (STRLK) and social media platform X.

data.title: First Commonwealth targets NIM in the low 4% range by Q4 2026 as swaps roll off and deposit rates are tested
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Commonwealth Financial Corporation (FCF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Michael Price reported net income of $37.5 million and $0.37 of EPS, adding that net interest income declined to $109.3 million as “we sold $210 million of Eastern PA commercial loans and loan balances fell another $74.2 million due to heightened payoffs.”
  • Price said commercial loan repayments rose to $630 million in Q1 and highlighted CRE resolution activity: “In the first quarter, we had 18 successful CRE projects. They were refinanced or sold, representing a payoff of approximately $240 million in loan outstandings.”
  • Price said the NIM “fell as expected to 3.92%” and pointed to catalysts: “positive replacement yields on new fixed rate loans in the first quarter were 54 basis points higher and coupled with $150 million of swaps rolling off in the second quarter, this should provide the impetus for further NIM expansion.”
  • Price described balance sheet actions and capital return, saying the company “paid off virtually all borrowings, lowered our loan-to-deposit ratio to 91% and grew tangible book value per share by 4.3% while at the same time repurchasing our stock.”
  • CFO James Reske attributed spread income pressure to calendar and rate impacts: “Spread income was down from last quarter by $4.2 million, but approximately $2.6 million of this decline can be attributed to having fewer days in the quarter.”
  • Reske updated the rate-cut base case and margin trajectory: “Based on our new one cut base case, we are revising our previous NIM guidance upwards slightly, about 3 to 5 basis points higher each quarter than before, drifting upwards to the low 4% range by the fourth quarter of this year.”
  • Price announced an IT leadership transition: “after 37 years at our company, Norm Montgomery, our Chief Information Officer, is retiring… and we have hired Ryan Gorney to replace Norm.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide EPS or revenue guidance in the transcript.
  • Reske revised NIM expectations upward, stating the company now sees NIM “drifting upwards to the low 4% range by the fourth quarter of this year,” supported by fewer expected rate cuts and the expiration of “$150 million of macro swaps on May 1 this Friday.”
  • Reske guided quarterly expenses and fees, saying, “We expect NIE per quarter to hover in the $74 million to $76 million range this year,” and “Fee income should range from $24 million to $25 million per quarter this year.”
  • On deposit pricing, Price said, “we are well positioned to test lower deposit rates in the next several quarters,” while emphasizing promotions have also been “a terrific way to gain new checking accounts.”

Financial Results

  • Price said noninterest expenses increased “$1.2 million to $75.5 million” and included “$500,000 of prepayment fees for the repurchase of long-term debt,” with the efficiency ratio rising to “55.4%.”
  • Price said the provision for loan losses increased “$3.7 million to $10.7 million” and tied the increase to “$9.6 million in specific reserves for 3 larger credits.”
  • Price said asset quality remained pressured: “Our nonperforming loans or NPLs to loans remained stubbornly high at 0.98%,” and noted that “of our $92.3 million in NPLs, $28.1 million or 30.4% is guaranteed by the SBA.”
  • Reske detailed capital actions and shareholder returns: “We repurchased approximately $22.7 million in stock last quarter at a weighted average price of $17.67,” added “We have $25 million remaining in repurchase authorization,” and said, “We announced a $0.02 increase in the dividend yesterday.”
  • Reske reported tangible book value per share “grew from $11.22 to $11.34,” and capital ratios of “CET1… 12.5%” and “TCE… 9.7%.”

Q&A

  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: asked whether charge-offs were tied to sold/paid-off credits and how provision/charge-offs could trend; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Brian Sohocki said, “we recorded $2.8 million during the fourth quarter when we moved them to held for sale,” and “there was approximately $400,000 that had paid off at par that were reversed,” adding, “We're not seeing any indicators of systematic stress across the portfolio.”
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked about elevated payoffs and loan-growth capacity; CEO Price said production was “well over $900 million,” while payoffs rose “from about $480 million to about $630 million,” and added, “we do feel good about the level of activity and that we can hit the guidance that we've given historically of mid-single loan growth.”
  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: pressed on exit margin and deposit tactics; CFO Reske said, “We think the fourth quarter should be over 4%,” but “the big variability… has been deposit behavior,” citing a “90.9%” loan-to-deposit ratio as giving room to “bring down our deposits.”
  • Karl Shepard, RBC: asked about the new “one cut” base case and provision direction; CFO Reske said the cut assumption was “late summer,” and CEO Price responded to provision drifting down: “I think so, yes.”
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked if buyback pace changes with loan growth; CFO Reske said, “It's not really driven… by the loan growth,” and described operating under guidance to return capital “up to the dollar amount of capital generation.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: asked about office maturities and excess cash; Sohocki said management evaluates maturities “throughout the whole portfolio” and is “actively pursuing exit,” while Reske said cash was being held for expected “public funds” outflows and could shift into “buying more securities” if loan growth does not materialize.
  • Daniel Cardenas, Brean: asked about customer sentiment and tax rate; Price said, “It might be too early to tell,” citing debit card interchange “off a couple of hundred thousand dollars,” while Reske said the quarter’s tax rate was “20.26%.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts) as questions repeatedly probed credit normalization, office maturities, and whether provision could “drift back down,” alongside multiple challenges on NIM durability.
  • Neutral to slightly positive (management) in prepared remarks, with Price calling out “further NIM expansion,” deposit growth, and “virtually all borrowings” repaid; in Q&A, management leaned measured, emphasizing variability (“the big variability… has been deposit behavior”) and isolated-credit framing (“We're not seeing any indicators of systematic stress”).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management’s tone shifted from highlighting strong profitability metrics and margin expansion to emphasizing payoff-driven balance sheet shrinkage and “stubbornly high” NPLs, while analysts maintained a consistent focus on margin path and credit outcomes.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized heightened payoffs, loan sales, and credit specifics, with Price highlighting $630 million of repayments and Reske noting NIM of 3.92% “in line with our previous guidance;” Q4 2025 emphasized stronger operating metrics including “core EPS of $0.43,” NIM “3.98%,” and a “core efficiency ratio of 52.8%.”
  • Guidance language on margin became more constructive in Q1 2026, as Reske said NIM guidance was revised “upwards slightly” on a “one cut base case,” versus Q4 2025 describing an expected “near-term dip… followed by gradual improvement… ending the year 2026 at around 4%.”
  • Analyst focus remained centered on NIM mechanics, deposit costs, buybacks, and credit, but Q1 2026 added sharper scrutiny on office maturities and payoff-driven loan growth headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Price said asset quality pressure persisted with NPLs at 0.98% and noted that “3 previously discussed relationships totaling $20.5 million moved to nonperforming status,” with Sohocki adding resolution would be “more of a slow ramp down to the historical level.”
  • Price flagged earnings pressure from payoffs and loan sales, and management discussed deposit-rate changes cautiously, with Price saying, “we'll pick our spots as we decrease rates,” balancing pricing with account growth.
  • Reske highlighted a near-term liquidity planning constraint tied to “public funds” outflows in Q2, describing why the company is holding elevated cash.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 around payoff-driven loan pressure, higher provision tied to a small number of larger credits, and a more liquid balance sheet that could enable lower deposit rates. At the same time, they raised the NIM path modestly on fewer expected rate cuts and the May expiration of $150 million of swaps, while keeping quarterly expense and fee-income ranges in place and continuing capital return through repurchases and an increased dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold extends ‌losses to one-month low, now down 12% since Iran war started
data.description:
Gold futures fell for the third consecutive session Wednesday ahead of the U.S. Federal Reserve's decision to keep interest rates unchanged, matching traders' expectations; prices continued to slip after-hours following the decision.

Market attention remains focused on U.S.-Iran mediation efforts, and gold prices have tumbled more than 12% since the war began—after surging 65% in 2025—as rising energy prices caused by the cutoff of shipments via the Strait of Hormuz have revived inflation fears, leaving the Fed with less room to cut rates.

Because gold does not pay interest, higher-for-longer rates would make it less attractive as investors can earn more elsewhere.

"Only Trump and Iran can rescue markets, but the two sides are not getting any closer to a deal, and oil is reflecting that," Forex.com's Fawad Razaqzada said in a note. "Against this backdrop, the gold outlook is not looking so bright right now."

Geopolitical risks are not limited to the Middle East war, World Gold Council analyst Louise Street said in a note, with pressure also stemming from global trade tensions, uncertainty over U.S. monetary policy, and the war in Ukraine.

Meanwhile, the longer the Middle East conflict persists, the greater the potential risk to global growth and wider economic spillovers, which would typically strengthen demand for gold as a safe-haven asset, Street said.

The WGC said global gold demand rose 2% Y/Y in Q1, as a surge in purchases of gold bars and coins, as well ⁠as a ​3% growth in buying by central banks, outweighed a ​23% decline in jewelry demand.

Goldman Sachs maintains its bullish outlook for gold, saying it continues to forecast prices to reach $5,400/oz by year's end, as geopolitical uncertainty and concerns over fiscal sustainability could accelerate global diversification into gold, reinforcing its long-term upward trajectory.

The bank said it expects speculative positioning to normalize and anticipates ~50 bps of rate cuts from the Fed, reducing the opportunity cost of holding gold.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for May delivery fell 1% to $4,545.20/oz, and front-month Comex May silver (XAGUSD:CUR) dropped 2.2% to $71.569/oz, the lowest settlement value for both precious metals since March 30.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
